title: On the Optimal Design of a Financial Stability Fund
abstract: Abstract We develop a model of a Financial Stability Fund (the “Fund” henceforth) for a union of sovereign countries. By design, the contract prevents country defaults, as well as undesired expected losses, which in a union translate into excessive risk mutualizations. A participant country has greater ability to borrow and share risks than using sovereign debt financing. The Fund contract also provides better incentives for the country to reduce endogenous risks. These efficiency gains arise from the ability of the Fund to offer long-term contingent financial contracts, subject to limited enforcement and moral hazard constraints. We develop the theory and quantitatively compare the constrained-efficient Fund economy with an incomplete markets economy with default. We calibrate our economy to the euro area “stressed countries” in the debt crisis (2010–2). Substantial welfare gains are achieved, particularly in times of crisis. The Fund is, in fact, a risk-sharing, crisis prevention and resolution mechanism, which transforms the participant countries’ defaultable sovereign debt into the union’s safe assets. In sum, our theory can help to improve current official lending practices and, for example, to eventually design a European Fiscal Fund.
summary_en: Could the euro area share risk across member states without tripping over the "no-bailout" political trap? This paper designs a Financial Stability Fund — long-term, state-contingent loans subject to participation and moral-hazard constraints — and calibrates it to the 2010–12 crisis countries (Greece, Ireland, Italy, Portugal, Spain). A fund of this kind delivers substantial welfare gains, especially during crises, because it can extend more credit and absorb more risk than sovereign bonds while still disciplining borrowers. The paper shows a constructive middle path between full mutualisation (eurobonds) and strict no-bailout rules.
data_used: Quantitative theoretical framework calibrated to euro-area sovereign data 2010–12 for the five "stressed" programme countries (GIIPS). Macro aggregates on output, debt, default risk and sovereign spreads are used for calibration; no new micro-data.
policy_relevance: For Hungarian officials engaged in EU fiscal-architecture debates: the paper offers a concrete, incentive-compatible alternative to both eurobonds and strict no-bailout doctrines. Relevant to the Ministry of Finance and MNB positions on ESM reform, EU fiscal capacity, and how a non-euro EU member should evaluate proposals. Caveat: the framework is calibrated to programme-country parameters and the welfare gains are model-based; institutional feasibility in the EU political process is a separate question.
